questions.optionB: Fractures involving the growth plate (physis) in children — 5 types based on fracture pattern through growth plate, epiphysis, and metaphysis
questions.explanation: Core concept: Salter-Harris = physeal (growth plate) fracture classification in children. Why correct: Type I: through physis only (widened physis). II: through physis + metaphysis (most common, good prognosis). III: through physis + epiphysis (intra-articular). IV: through metaphysis + physis + epiphysis (needs anatomical reduction). V: crush injury to physis (worst prognosis — growth arrest). Why A wrong: Adult fractures classified by AO/Weber/Garden etc. Why C wrong: Spinal fractures — Denis three-column, TLICS. Why D wrong: Pathological fractures — by underlying cause. Clinical relevance: Types III-V involve epiphysis/joint → risk of growth arrest, angular deformity. Treatment principle: anatomical reduction of physis to prevent growth disturbance. Exam trap: SALTR mnemonic: Slip, Above, Lower, Through, Rammed (crush). Type II most common (~75%). Memory aid: 'SALTR: S=Slipped (I), A=Above (II-metaphysis), L=Lower (III-epiphysis), T=Through (IV-all), R=Rammed (V-crushed).' Reference: Maheshwari – Pediatric Orthopedics.
questions.conceptTag: Salter-Harris physeal growth plate fracture children

questions.questionText: The most reliable sign of a fracture on clinical examination is:
questions.optionB: Crepitus (grating sensation felt when fractured bone ends rub together — pathognomonic for fracture but should NOT be deliberately elicited due to pain and potential neurovascular damage)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Crepitus = pathognomonic for fracture (but don't deliberately elicit). Why correct: Crepitus: grating/crackling felt when broken bone ends move against each other. Pathognomonic sign. Also: abnormal mobility at fracture site is equally pathognomonic. Why A wrong: Swelling can occur with soft tissue injury without fracture. Why C wrong: Tenderness is sensitive but not specific (sprains, contusions). Why D wrong: Loss of function can be from pain alone without fracture. Clinical relevance: Other fracture signs: deformity, shortening, abnormal mobility, crepitus. X-ray confirms. Neurovascular assessment mandatory before and after manipulation. Exam trap: NEVER deliberately test crepitus — causes pain, additional tissue damage. Memory aid: 'Crepitus = Cracked bone (confirmed).' Reference: Maheshwari – General Fractures.
